title: Gender roles and the cast
wikitext:
[]: Gender roles and the cast             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Wed Jun 09, 2010 6:11 pm After experiencing the magic of moonspeak feminist academia , some of us have been talking about...things. May Yui-sama eat me if this has been done before, but I was inspired by this: Stuart wrote: ...the female characters in positions of authority, Ritsuko and Misato, end up showing emotion in the end, cracking up, revealing their vulnerabilities, whereas Fuyutsuki and Gendo remain cool and calm throughout. Misato is also aligned with the home and the private sphere for Shinji as she lets him live with her, whereas Gendo is only ever portrayed doing business. Even in flashbacks with Yui he was always on business.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Wed Jun 09, 2010 7:55 pm I'll just leave this here. Yoshiyuki Sadamoto wrote: The character design request from Anno was that "the lead character is a girl, and has an older-sister type figure like Coach next to her," so it was structurally similar to "Gunbuster". So I first designed an Asuka-type girl as the lead character, but after "Gunbuster" and "Nadia" I felt some resistance to making the lead character a girl again. I mean a robot should be piloted by a trained person, and if that person just happens to be a girl then that is fine, but I couldn't see why a young girl would pilot a robot... So I remember saying to Anno, "It's a robot story, so let's make the lead character a boy."

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Jun 09, 2010 8:01 pm Gendo lets loose a little emotion at the end, admitting his personal faults and mistakes as a father. Shinji can behave in a manly fashion when the need arises, such as confronting gendo in 19. Asuka becomes a girly-girl when Kaji walks by Yeah, I'd say things are somewhat up in the air.

[]: Re: Gender roles and the cast             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Wed Jun 09, 2010 8:46 pm oOoOoOo wrote: Let's talk about gender! Thoughts on Stuart-kun's utterance? Do the characters of Evangelion fall neatly into "masculine" and "feminine", or do they transgress and move between?

[]:             Quote Post by TheLobe » Wed Jun 09, 2010 9:56 pm Shinji Asuka and Rei where each designed by gender swapping an anime stereotype, which is why they don't fit as clearly as gendo and Misato. That said Ritsuko Misato and Kaji are a reflection of them, as I suppose are the bridge bunnies and Gendo naoko and Yui.

[]:             Quote Post by Stuart » Wed Jun 09, 2010 11:23 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak, it's interesting how you think of Gendo as asexual. How so? To me he is very much the man figure, the father, the head of an organisation, devoted to his work at the cost of ignoring his son. Perhaps a figure of a specifically Japanese workaholic father? With Yui, the fan interpretations of how her soul ended up in Eva vary between unfortunate accident to wilful act of self-sacrifice to ensure a certain outcome to instrumentality. These readings cast her either as a passive victim of (masculine, man made) technology, or as an active subverter of this masculine technology. In episode 19 when Ritsuko describes Unit1 as breaking free from its man-made shackles, are we seeing the "natural" side of the Eva, its body (with nature aligned with the feminine) breaking free of patriarchal control? The result is that Eva goes beserk; is the message that women cannot be civilised without the control of men? Or is this a righteous rage of indignation against the male oppressor? When Shinj gets swallowed into the Eva, he is able to come back of his own volition. Not so Yui, which would make me think that she is cast more as helpless and passive in this case, awaiting to be brought back to life the active, ever working Gendo, just as we expect female characters to be "rescued" by men in all sorts of stories.

[]: Re: Gender roles and the cast             Quote Post by Synapsid » Thu Jun 10, 2010 12:31 am oOoOoOo wrote: Let's talk about gender! Thoughts on Stuart-kun's utterance? Do the characters of Evangelion fall neatly into "masculine" and "feminine", or do they transgress and move between?...Anno-san certainly is aware of gender, and what his target audience is expecting.

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Thu Jun 10, 2010 2:24 am This is an awesome topic that I'm extremely interested in and close to. I think it'll lead to some truly wonderful discussion. I just want to make sure we're all on the page on something because, as our OP says, it'll inevitably lead to a shitstorm. This thread isn't about genderbending so let's leave all that sort of thing in the Shinko thread. No traps, no reverse-traps, no anti-traps, quasi-traps, semi, hemi, or demi-traps (of course I'm only referring to Eva characters, our good friends Xeroko and Synapsid are more than welcome). I'm not calling anyone out about this, only issuing a preemptive reminder. So there. Now that I've stamped this thread with the Official EGF Staff Member Seal of Anality, you guys can't get mad if I have to yell at you in this thread. Anyway, this thread's off to a good start and has a ton of potential so let's keep it going.

[]:             Quote Post by Stuart » Thu Jun 10, 2010 2:40 am Lovely jubbly! Just a quick reply on the question of Asuka; is she manly in her competitive, boisterous and impetuous nature, or is this just yet another stereotyped female, "the fiery redhead"?

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Thu Jun 10, 2010 3:29 am This thread is certainly interesting, but I won't probably have a lot to add to the discussion. However, there's one thing I'd like to point out: Stuart wrote: These readings cast her either as a passive victim of (masculine, man made) technology, or as an active subverter of this masculine technology.

[]:             Quote Post by BC Baron » Thu Jun 10, 2010 3:54 am Haven't posted in quite a while and I'm not really sure I'll have anything valuable to contribute here. However, for what it's worth... While I in no way claim to know what Anno's intentions were or have any specific 'behind the scenes' quotes to support my arguments, I figured that since the show originally hit the airwaves back around 1995 and the story is supposed to take place circa 2015, perhaps Anno was simply trying to suggest that rigidly defined gender stereotypes would no longer have a place in society twenty years or so down the line.

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Thu Jun 10, 2010 4:34 am @Synapsid: That was a very illuminating post and not the least bit tumorous. You don't need to be so self-effacing.

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Thu Jun 10, 2010 6:49 am I'm happy things have gone so well here. I'll reply to more things later, I just wanted to touch on Stuart-kun's Asuka question: ...is she manly in her competitive, boisterous and impetuous nature, or is this just yet another stereotyped female, "the fiery redhead"?

[]: Re: Gender roles and the cast             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Jun 10, 2010 7:13 am I second whatever Zap said about this topic. Bear-paw stamp of approval added to Zap's 'Anal' stamp. oOoOoOo wrote: Let's talk about gender! Thoughts on Stuart-kun's utterance? Do the characters of Evangelion fall neatly into "masculine" and "feminine", or do they transgress and move between?

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Thu Jun 10, 2010 7:46 am I can actually dig this thread. You have the Ran seal of approval that my points on various aspects of academic feminism won't be riddled with trolls. This might also prove for some delicious dialogues for the upcoming Wiki dialogue section so I'll be watching this thread very closely. Stuart wrote: ObsessiveMathsFreak, it's interesting how you think of Gendo as asexual. How so? To me he is very much the man figure, the father, the head of an organisation, devoted to his work at the cost of ignoring his son. Perhaps a figure of a specifically Japanese workaholic father?

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Jun 10, 2010 8:14 am Gendo as a phallic symbol? Not so sure about that one. As for men being better outfitted for physical survival- those huge muscles aren't always good, especially when they're being built at the cost of your immune system. Testosterone hammers white blood cell counts. Also, Yui saves her son each time she goes berserk. There is no lasting harm done in any of the berserk incidents due to her going berserk. That being said, it's worth noting that she is the most powerful character in the show in the end. Your comparison of Fuyutsuki to a medieval knight is interesting, I must admit.

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Thu Jun 10, 2010 6:21 pm Gendo seems quintessentially masculine. He doesn't cry or get emotional and keeps everything bottled inside. He threw away all the things that reminded him of Yui (except for what was in his heart), and makes no effort to communicate his woe to anyone. (A woman supposedly makes a "nest" by collecting and decorating.) Like, even when he faces Yui at the end of everything, he's like "Yeah, I guess I was a bit of a jerk" and then he gets eaten. Like, he even hides the fact that he's got his feet in water, in that one scene, and everyone thinks he is so stoic and calm. What a man . ~ahem~ This thread has actually burst my mind. Too much to think about at once. I don't know what to add to Synpasy-kun's novella. ^^; I think what you say about Rei slowly coming to reject Gendo is important, though. Yui also seems to reject Gendo, and I think the idea of her breaking free of the man -made technology that restrains her is right on. But perhaps it is better to say she's breaking free of Gendo, a lot like Rei does... who is sort of Yui to begin with. None of Gendo's plans go quite right, and he treats both Yui and Rei as tools (even if his end goal is lovey-dovey, he isn't exactly considering what Yui and Rei truly want). He's pretty much the patriarch, although... he does control everyone. The women under his control all rebel (actually, I think everyone under his care rebels). Ritsuko is another example. But the wacky Gendo/Yui/Shinji/Rei family unit seems like it could be a critique of the usual gender roles. Gendo's got the master plan and everyone has to help. Then they tell him to fuck off and die. Breakdown of the family! Gnnnaarrrr!!!

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Thu Jun 10, 2010 6:51 pm Stuart wrote: ObsessiveMathsFreak, it's interesting how you think of Gendo as asexual. How so? To me he is very much the man figure, the father, the head of an organisation, devoted to his work at the cost of ignoring his son. Perhaps a figure of a specifically Japanese workaholic father?

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jun 10, 2010 8:13 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: I like the idea of Fuyutsuki as a kind of knight as well, but it probably ignores the fact that he'd one of the few characters in Evangelion with a direct and uncomplicated love interest (in someone of the opposite sex).

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Jun 10, 2010 9:44 pm Compared to what the others are facing, yes.
